description: The end uses that pay for materials engineering today and that programmable matter will reshape over 30 years.
examples: Drug design · semiconductor process integration · battery cell chemistry · aerospace alloys · cell therapy scaffolds · food substrates · regenerative medicine
notes: These are the buyers Lupine sells to today (and they are real revenue) — but they are also the surfaces that get reshaped when phases 2-4 of the arc land.

description: Foundation models that propose novel matter configurations from a target property specification.
examples: DeepMind GNoME · MatterSim · OMat24 · GNN crystal generators · diffusion models for molecular structures
notes: This layer is real and ramping fast. Lupine is not building it; we host it. Our wager is that this layer fails without a layer below it — which is us.

description: Methodology + tooling that names where the layers above fail and compresses that failure into the layers below.
examples: Lupine atlas-distill (Rust) · sloppy-model geometry · multi-fidelity UQ · Pearl causal inference · Simpson's-paradox detection · OpenKIM-grade benchmarks · IMMI 2026 paper
notes: This is the layer Lupine is. It is structurally underweight in the current ecosystem — every other layer is racing ahead while validation gets ad-hoc. We bet that the audit layer becomes a discipline of its own, the way observability did for software.

description: Numerical primitives the audit and generative layers stand on.
examples: Plane-wave DFT (VASP / QE / ABINIT / GPAW) · LAMMPS-class MD · ML-potential inference (MACE/NequIP/Allegro) · WebGPU / GPU acceleration · cloud HPC
notes: Lupine is interoperable with all of these and will ship an open Rust implementation of the canonical primitives. The compute layer is mature in legacy form, immature in modern form.

description: The ground truth: experiments, synthesis, characterization.
examples: Beamlines · electron microscopes · X-ray diffraction · synthesis robotics · DOE user facilities · self-driving labs
notes: The thing being modeled. The audit layer is the only place where the predictions and the experiments meet — that is structurally why this layer matters.
